scenario: You called a BTN open from the BB with 7♥6♥ (capped range — you'd 3-bet your premiums). Flop 8♠5♦2♣ — you have an open-ended straight draw. PFR c-bets.
question: As the OOP caller with a draw facing a c-bet, best option?
options: Check/call — you have a real draw (OESD, 8 outs) with implied odds, and your range is capped so raising bloats vs their initiative
explanation: As the pre-flop caller you're OOP with a capped range and no initiative. With a genuine draw (OESD), check/calling is standard — you have the odds to continue and keep your range disguised. Hardin lists your OOP options as donk/check-fold/check-call/check-raise; with a drawing hand and no made value, check-call is the disciplined default.

scenario: You called a CO open in position on the BTN with A♦J♦. Flop J♣8♠3♥ — top pair good kicker. PFR c-bets.
question: In position as the caller with TPGK facing a c-bet, plan?
options: Call — you have a strong one-pair hand in position; calling keeps their bluffs/worse Jx in, lets you control the pot and realize equity with position
explanation: As the IP caller, your options vs a c-bet are fold/call/raise. With TPGK, calling is usually better than raising: you keep their c-bet bluffs and worse Jx in the pot, you have position to control later streets, and raising mostly folds out worse and bloats vs an uncapped aggressor range. Note your range is capped (no AA/KK/AK — you'd 3-bet those), so play it as the strong-one-pair hand it is, in position.

scenario: You called an open from the BB with 4♦4♥ (set-mining). Flop K♠Q♦7♣ — you missed your set, have a pair of fours (under everything). PFR c-bets.
question: You set-mined 44, missed, now have bottom pair on a high board. Action?
options: Check/call — pair of fours has value
Check/fold — you set-mined and missed; on K-Q-7 your 44 beats nothing in their c-betting range, and you have no draw
explanation: Set-mining is the WHOLE plan with small pairs — if you miss the set, you're done. On K-Q-7, pair of fours is essentially the same as no pair: their c-betting range (Kx, Qx, overpairs, broadway) has you beaten, and you have no draw. Check/fold. The implied-odds reason to call preflop evaporates the moment you don't flop the set. Don't get attached to a tiny pair.

scenario: YOUR HAND. You over-limped T♠8♠ on the BTN behind a limper. Flop A♠T♣3♥ — middle pair (tens). You check it through. Turn 8♣ — now two pair (T+8). You bet, one call. River J♠. Villain leads big into you.
question: As the limped-pot caller with turned two pair, villain leads big on the J river. Read?
options: Caution/fold-lean — the J completes KQ/QT straights, the ace-high flop means Ax two pair beats you; a passive player leading big on the river rarely bluffs. Your two pair is a bluff-catcher at best
explanation: Your real T8s hand. Playing as the caller in a limped multiway pot, you made a vulnerable two pair. The river J completes obvious straights and your tens-and-eights loses to any Ax two pair and all straights. A passive limper leading big into you is screaming value at micro. Two pair FEELS strong but its relative strength here is weak — bluff-catcher at best. This over-limp set up a hand that makes second-best hands — the leak started preflop.

scenario: You call a BTN open from the BB with K♥9♥. Flop K♣7♠2♦ — top pair, weak kicker. PFR c-bets small. You're OOP.
question: OOP caller, top pair weak kicker facing a small c-bet. Best line?
options: Check-call — TPWK has showdown value but a weak kicker; calling keeps their bluffs in and controls the pot OOP; raising bloats vs a capped-but-uncapped aggressor and folds out worse
explanation: As the OOP caller with top-pair-weak-kicker, check-calling is the disciplined line. You have showdown value worth continuing, but the weak kicker means you don't want to build a big pot — check-raising bloats the pot OOP with a one-pair hand and folds out the bluffs you beat. Call, control the pot, reassess on the turn. Don't turn a medium hand into a big-pot hand from out of position.

scenario: You call a CO open on the BTN with A♠5♠. Flop 9♠6♠2♣ — flush draw + ace overcard. PFR c-bets. You're IN position.
question: IP caller with the nut flush draw facing a c-bet. Best line?
options: Call OR raise as a semi-bluff — nut flush draw with position is a premium draw; calling keeps their range wide and disguised, raising adds fold equity. Both fine; calling is lower-variance
explanation: As the IP caller with the nut flush draw, you have a premium drawing hand and position — both calling and semi-bluff raising are valid. Calling realizes equity cheaply, keeps their bluffs in, and disguises your hand; raising adds fold equity and can win it now. At micro vs a c-betting range, calling is the lower-variance default and lets you stack off when you hit the nuts. Position makes draws much more playable — you control the pot and see free cards when checked to.
